data.title: Zepp Health: A Riskier Bet As Gross Margins Compress (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The S&P 500 has skyrocketed to fresh all-time highs, and many value-oriented investors like myself are wondering the same thing: are there still good value stocks to buy? In my view, now is an excellent time to be an active investor, pruning positions from our portfolios that no longer meet the bill in order to redeploy capital into unloved sectors, particularly in the software space, where many bargains abound.

Zepp Health (ZEPP), a Chinese smartwatch company, is a name I'm gradually souring on. Though the stock has become more of a value play after a ~40% slide this year, I'm becoming more nervous around the company's inability to turn a profit. In a more risk-averse market environment where more investors are demanding to see tech companies' profits, I'm no longer certain there's a light at the end of Zepp Health's pain.

I last wrote a buy article on Zepp in November, when the stock was trading at $24 per share. Since then, Zepp has nosedived, and though the stock has been a blight in my portfolio, I'm losing the patience to see through a turnaround in this name. Though I still consider Zepp's growth to be best-in-class, I'm dropping my rating on the stock to neutral.

At current share prices, I see more of a balanced bull and bear case for this name. On the bright side for this company:

  • Zepp's Amazfit lineup is achieving substantial growth for the company. Revenue is growing north of >40% y/y, as the company is buoyed by successful recent product launches. Compare this to mid-teens growth for the fitness division at Garmin (GRMN), one of its major rivals, and ~flat performance for the wearables, home, and accessories segment at Apple (AAPL), which doesn't provide a specific breakout for the Apple Watch.
  • Striking high-end partnerships that are legitimizing its product in the fitness community. The company just signed a three-year deal with HYROX, an athletic race that is gaining substantial fans and attention globally. This partnership not only elevates Amazfit's brand in the fitness community, but could also see the development of more fitness technology beyond watches, such as rings and tracking straps.

At the same time, however, I am concerned about the following factors:

  • Zepp is facing tough competition this year, and the macro setup doesn't help. The company is coming off the back of major product launches in 2025 that will make it difficult to maintain double-digit growth, especially in a tougher macro environment where more consumers may choose to hold onto their watches for longer.
  • Relatively weak margin profile. The company maintains gross margins in the high 30s, which have been battered by tariffs and are ~20 points below Garmin. This severely hamstrings Zepp's ability to scale.

From a valuation standpoint, we note that, at least on a revenue basis, Zepp trades at cheaper revenue multiples than its closest pure-play peer, Garmin:

That said, profit is a different story. Zepp's razor-thin margins lead to a low denominator from an EV/EBITDA perspective, and against the bottom line, it's Garmin that looks like the relative bargain. We think that without a meaningful change to Zepp's gross margin profile, its ability to scale will be limited.

All in all, with the plethora of high-quality software businesses for sale right now amid the "SaaSpocalypse" trend, there's no need to strain ourselves to find growth-at-a-reasonable-price stocks. Zepp has lost its appeal; in my view, it's time to start trimming this position.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Zepp's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Zepp's revenue grew 43% y/y to $85.2 million. While this came in on the higher end of the company's guidance range of 38-45% y/y growth for the quarter, I'm disappointed in the revenue results, as the company had cited Q3 (which grew at 78% y/y, buoyed by the launch of the new $399 T-Rex Pro rugged smartwatch) was constrained by both typhoon conditions in China and supply constraints on the $99 Helio Strap that pushed some orders out from Q3 to Q4. I had hoped that, combined, these two factors would have pushed more revenue to Q4 and blunted the deceleration between the two quarters.

In Q4, Zepp's gross margin rose 360bps y/y to 40.4%, which the company billed as an all-time record. The company notes that the margin improvement was achieved due to the fact that the company hit strong holiday-quarter sales without heavy discounting, which is a big plus.

That said, quarterly gross margins can be influenced by product mix, especially as Zepp's product launch timing is more irregular. When we smooth this out over the course of the year, we are more concerned about the company's 38.3% full-year margin, which fell -170bps y/y to 38.3% for Amazfit-only products (which is the company's focus going forward), and -20bps against the full product portfolio in FY24, which included some Xiaomi-branded sales.

Now, it's not all bad news for Zepp. In February (Q1), the company just launched the $550 T-Rex Ultra 2, which it billed as its highest-priced product ever. If sales are successful and Zepp/Amazfit can continue migrating customers toward these pricier watches, we could see gross margins on an upward glide path.

Per CEO Wang Huang's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In our Premium portfolio, the T-Rex and Balance series continue to perform strongly. In February, we launched T-Rex Ultra 2, our newest flagship outdoor watch, built with Grade 5 titanium and designed for achieve durability, Ultra 2 extends the top end of our portfolio to around the USD 550 price level. The highest price point in our history. Products like Ultra 2 reinforce the premium positioning of the Amazfit brand while expanding the selling of our product portfolio."

The other good news is that the company has been able to offset gross margin headwinds with opex reductions. In FY25, opex fell to 47.8% of revenue, a ~13 point reduction from 60.5% in FY24, as the company achieved economies of scale on its much larger sales base on R&D and sales and marketing costs.

Still, we worry about overall profitability: despite margin improvements, in Q4 the company's EBIT was still negative at -$3.0 million; while full-year EBIT was -$24.4 million (a -40% smaller loss than FY24, but perhaps not enough appease investors in a more risk-averse market).

Key takeaways

I've been disappointed with Zepp's recent share price performance, but I'm no longer quite as confident that the company's strong revenue growth trends and market share gains in smartwatches justify an upward re-rating for this stock without the company proving that it has a path to richer gross margins. This is especially true when rival Garmin has guided to a 58.5% gross margin in FY26, or twenty points richer than Zepp (which justifies a major chunk of the revenue multiple premium against Zepp):

In my view, it's best to move to the sidelines on Zepp for now, until we get confirmation on stronger sales for higher-end products that can move the margin profile higher and push Zepp to positive EBIT.

data.title: USA Compression Partners: A High-Yielding Income Idea
data.description:
The last time I co-wrote an article was years ago when USA Compression Systems (USAC) became part of the Energy Transfer (ET) family of companies. To this day, Energy Transfer owns the general partner and so controls the daily operations of the company. This partnership has long sold itself as an income idea. With the expected recovery of natural gas prices, a company like this may well benefit from the steadily increasing volume of natural gas production expected in the future.

There are many areas of oil production that become gassier as the production ages. This introduces a countercyclical element to the equation that offsets (somewhat) the downside potential of any company that operates in this industry.

On the other hand, anything involving oil and gas is cyclical in nature. That introduces an element of risk that may not be acceptable to some income investors. To counter that cyclical nature, this company tends to market its products towards parts of the market that are not cyclical. One example of this would be natural gas power generators. That business is not only longer term in nature than is the case with upstream. But it also does not really have a cyclical nature to it.

This marketing strategy underpins the income idea for the common units. The success of the income model is very dependent upon the success of that marketing strategy.

What helps here is that this is a very large organization. That size enables some differentiation in the structure to service different ideas for the use of its products that smaller competitors cannot deal with. As a result, this is one of the few income ideas in the compression industry that has succeeded (at least so far).

Performance History

The current five-year history benefits from the general upstream recovery that took place after fiscal year 2020. Long-term readers may well remember that at one point, the United States production actually decreased 3 million BOD (very roughly) due to the COVID challenges.

That production came back online towards the end of fiscal year 2020 and later, as oil prices again became positive and later rallied toward very high prices in fiscal year 2022.

For the five-year history that is being presented throughout the industry (in various forms), the result is an unusually attractive five-year record, as shown above. That result is much more likely to return towards the long-term return here of mostly distributions with a little bit of appreciation.

Going forward, it is reasonable to expect a nominal distribution increase each year along with some sort of single-digit common unit price appreciation potential. If there is a period of inflation ahead, this may not be the vehicle to keep up with an inflationary trend.

On the other hand, this company made it through fiscal year 2020 in very reasonable shape. That is in contrast to much of the industry that suffered from a whole lot of returns of compressors as production was shut-in when oil prices briefly went negative.

Even though there is a bit of risk to the income idea here, the fact that this partnership survived fiscal year 2020 shows that the marketing idea made a material difference from much of the industry to make the income idea survive a challenge like fiscal year 2020. I followed many upstream related ideas that did not make it through that period.

Diversification

This company supports the total compressor cycle from manufacturing all the way through to the end of usage of the compressor.

The company is also diversified horizontally in that it services the major basins as shown above.

Between those two forms of diversification is a lot of safety that many in this rather fractured part of the oil & gas industry cannot demonstrate.

Debt

This also supports a bit more leverage than some other companies have in the industry. The leverage here stays in the 4.0 range for the debt ratio. The overall steadiness of the business makes that financial leverage work.

The result is that from a debt perspective, Energy Transfer manages this more like a midstream business than would be typical for a lot of compression companies (that have a materially lower debt ratio). As the debt ratings above demonstrate, the whole thing works and has worked for some time.

As a side note, this is a highly fractured industry with no one company having a commanding share of the market. Therefore, periodic acquisitions are the norm (and are likely to be the norm for some time to come). Such acquisitions can provide for some incremental share appreciation potential.

Summary

The distribution was last raised in 2015. Then again, that is a much better record than much of the industry (the compression industry) that cut the distribution back in 2020 when the going got really rough.

Going forward, it looks as though finances are finally such that there may be the ability to gradually increase that dividend (for the first time in some years).

Back when I cowrote the article (years ago), the distribution yield was well past 10%. The fact that it is now under 10% indicates some market certainty about the distribution that did not exist back then.

The thing to note is that most investors report an 8% compounded return long-term that is made up of mostly dividends. In this case, that whole return is the dividend. The difference now is that there is some reserve "just in case" another industry challenge appears.

Any stock price appreciation potential going forward is "icing on the cake". That appreciation potential should push the total return into the low teens. The above average appreciation of the stock price that happened after fiscal year 2020 is now largely over with.

However, there is expected to be some decent demand for compression systems as production becomes gassier in some basins (as unconventional production ages). The demand for compressors that comes with all the exporting projects is also a macro tailwind as well.

This is considered a strong buy idea (with some caveats) in that the compression industry is expected to benefit from increasing natural gas production and usage in the future. The caveats would be that the industry outlook needs to be monitored continuously, and the economy in general needs to be reviewed for the future outlook.

Risk

This income idea would be considered an elevated risk situation because the economic outlook is very important, and so is the natural gas pricing outlook. In the past, this partnership has made it through a lot of challenges without cutting the distribution. But there is no assurance that will not happen in the future.

Finances are much better than they were years ago when I cowrote the previous article. As long as that remains the case or the situation improves, then this idea remains in place. However, should finances deteriorate, I would exit the position before there is trouble (potentially).

data.title: The Trade Driving The S&P 500 Higher May Not Last
data.description:
Many of the mega-cap stocks have rallied significantly over the past week, driving the S&P 500 to outperform the equal-weighted S&P 500 ETF (RSP). This is an interesting development because it appears to be the same dispersion trade seen in previous quarters.

What makes the current period more notable is that the credit markets are not confirming the move witnessed in mega-cap technology stocks. Credit Default Swaps are not validating the move in some stocks to the same degree.

Dispersion May Be the Driver of the Market

This is evident in the S&P 500 dispersion index, which climbed to around 36.5 this past week, peaking at 38 on Wednesday, April 15. This indicates that stocks are expected to trade independently of one another. It is another way of saying market-implied correlations are low. For the most part, this rise in the DSPX is a seasonal pattern that has recurred, rising into earnings, consolidating around earnings, and then unwinding after earnings seasons.

The idea is that a trader or fund will go long single-stock implied volatility and short index-level implied volatility, best illustrated by comparing the S&P 500 VIX constituent index to the VIX index itself. What is clear is that index-level volatility has fallen much faster and further than constituent volatility, which creates the dispersion.

It would suggest that the market is still pricing in very high levels of implied volatility for single stocks and significantly lower for the index. Typically, once mega-cap stocks conclude their earnings announcements, the dispersion trade unwinds, pushing up implied correlations and causing the market to reverse gains.

One Way to Watch

The DSPX index minus the 3-month implied correlation index tends to do a good job of indicating when the trade is taking place and when the unwind is underway. On several occasions over the past few years, the spread has reached extremes like the one it is currently exhibiting. The last few examples of this can be seen in October 2025 and February 2026. This was also witnessed in February 2025 and August 2024.

The spread can help frame potential turning points in the S&P 500 over time. Recently, the spread has widened sharply, along with the rise in the index, suggesting the move may be becoming stretched.

Credit Not Convinced

Notably, there have been significant moves among some of the index's biggest names, which have helped drive the market-cap-weighted S&P 500 index's outperformance. But what also stands out is that while the stocks have rallied sharply, the credit markets' views have not evolved.

For example, Meta (META) has seen its stock surge to its highest price since February 3. Meanwhile, the 5-year credit default swap for Meta is trading at a wider spread than at the end of February.

Meanwhile, the same can be said of Oracle (ORCL), which has also risen significantly. Meanwhile, the 5-year CDS, while improving, appears not to have improved as significantly.

A number of reasons could drive the disconnect between stocks and credit; perhaps the CDS market is less liquid than the equity market, so prices evolve more slowly in credit. Perhaps the equity market became more bearish on the two stocks than it should have been, and they were simply oversold.

While this does not typically resolve immediately, the current setup is consistent with prior periods that led to consolidation or downside. This would suggest that the risk-reward for equities may be becoming less favorable as we approach and move through mega-cap earnings.

data.title: VTV: Vanguard's Passive $169B Value ETF Is Good, But SEIV's Active Approach May Be Better
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In typical Vanguard fashion, the Vanguard Value ETF (VTV) offers investors low-cost passive exposure to a broad basket of stocks that tend to perform reasonably well under all market conditions. My educational review of the fund demonstrated that VTV rarely delivered returns in the top or bottom quartiles compared to other large-cap value ETF peers, so in that respect, it's a pretty safe bet. However, this article explores the possibility of replacing VTV with the actively managed SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value Factor ETF (SEIV), which has an expense ratio of just 0.15% and better total returns since its inception on May 17, 2022.

As I'll demonstrate below, SEIV features a substantially discounted forward P/E ratio (11.44x vs. 17.08x), 1-4% more historical and estimated EPS growth, and quality features that are at least just as solid. Although more risky and not quite as diversified - SEIV holds 117 stocks compared to 309 for VTV - I think this tradeoff is worth it for the better factor exposure SEIV offers. Therefore, I've rated VTV a "hold" and SEIV a "buy," and I hope you enjoy this head-to-head analysis comparing the two.

VTV vs. SEIV: Important Strategy Differences Worth Noting

As I mentioned earlier, VTV is a passively managed, rules-based fund, while SEIV is actively managed and follows what I call a "single style" strategy, which is not to be confused with a "single factor" strategy. This means that SEIV won't necessarily hold value stocks that score poorly on other factors, namely growth, quality, and momentum. In this sense, it behaves more like a multi-factor ETF, but undoubtedly, the value lean is the strongest. That will become evident later when I highlight its fundamentals.

VTV, on the other hand, also applies a multi-factor screening process, but not in the same way. Since April 16, 2013, it has tracked the CRSP Large Cap Value Index, which is a float-adjusted, cap-weighted Index that draws from an Index comprised of the top 85% of the total U.S. market capitalization. The multi-factor models used separate value from growth, but importantly, the value stocks selected tend to rank poorly on the growth factors, and vice versa, based on the criteria below.

In practice, this means that VTV's ideal stocks would have low P/E and P/B ratios and high dividend yields, but also low sales and EPS growth rates. In addition, since ROA is a factor in the growth model, VTV could easily tilt away from quality. These potential growth and quality deficiencies are things that SEIV managers won't accept too much of, and that's evident when evaluating the two ETFs fundamentally. As shown below, SEIV has the advantage on 3Y EPS CAGR (6.50% vs. 5.72%), 1Y Estimated EPS Growth (14.12% vs. 10.02%), and ROA (8.29% vs. 6.69%).

Lastly, the table above highlights how much more popular VTV is compared to SEIV. Its AUM is enormous at $169B, and its 0.03% expense ratio is the cheapest among all the U.S. large-cap value ETFs I track. For its part, SEIV isn't bad either, with $1.2B in assets under management since its May 2022 launch. Furthermore, its 0.15% expense ratio is tied for second place among all actively managed U.S. large-cap value ETFs behind the WisdomTree US Value Fund (WTV). However, SEIV's 0.12% 30-day median bid/ask spread is elevated compared to VTV's 0.01%, so there are some differences in terms of liquidity to consider. Over the long run, I don't think this will matter much, but certainly if you decide to buy SEIV, I recommend using limit orders and avoiding buying at the market open, when spreads tend to be the widest.

VTV vs. SEIV: Performance Comparison

How VTV Wins By Not Losing

If you read my educational review of VTV, you might remember that it was one of only six U.S. large-cap value ETFs to never deliver fourth-quartile returns over the last ten years. The five others were:

  1. Schwab US Large-Cap Value ETF (SCHV)
  2. SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV)
  3. Schwab Fundamental US Broad Market ETF (FNDB)
  4. Vanguard Mega Cap Value ETF (MGV)
  5. WisdomTree U.S. Total Dividend Fund (DTD)

I don't want to understate the importance of "winning by not losing," and VTV is an excellent example of how you don't need big wins to succeed. Remember that a 20% decline requires a 25% recovery just to break even, and over the last ten years, VTV's worst year was in 2018, when it declined by 5.44%. That's manageable for most investors, and the result has been a compounded 211% total return since May 2016 that ranks #13/51 among all U.S. large-cap value ETFs with that ten-year history. For a complete list of these results, please refer to the table below.

VTV also ranks:

  • #18/51 on volatility (annualized standard deviation)
  • #18/51 on maximum drawdowns
  • #29/51 on "Best 12 Months" return
  • #20/51 on "Worst 12 Months" return
  • #13/51 on risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe Ratio)
  • #14/51 on downside risk-adjusted returns (Sortino Ratio)

These rankings reinforce VTV's "winning by not losing" profile. In short, it won't blow you away with huge one-time gains, but it also won't put your capital at too much risk, and that's proven to be a winning strategy over the long term.

SEIV: Track Record Is Short But Impressive

On the other hand, SEIV's four-year track record is likely too short to draw many meaningful conclusions. However, it's still impressive. As shown below, it's delivered a 77.38% total return since June 2022 compared to 54.56% for VTV, and even though its 13.21% max drawdown was about 3% more, it's demonstrated an ability to bounce back quickly. In fact, its "Worst 6 Months" return of -3.46% was actually 3% better than VTV's. This is a good reminder that unless one plans to frequently trade these ETFs based on current market conditions (which is not my suggestion), it's important to value the recoveries just as much as the drawdowns.

Still, it's reasonable to question if SEIV is simply benefitting from a favorable launch date. We know from the earlier statistics that it intentionally does not ignore other non-value factors, including momentum. And, given the unusual strength of the momentum factor over the last three years, SEIV's substantial outperformance over this period is perfectly explainable. However, it's difficult to say what will happen if the momentum factor falls out of favor for an extended period. We just don't have the performance data yet, so while I acknowledge SEIV shareholders are flying a little bit blind right now, I think we can still learn a lot about potential risks by evaluating its composition and fundamentals, so let's do that next.

VTV vs. SEIV: Sector Allocation Comparison

VTV and SEIV have substantially different sector compositions. I would say that with 22.25% allocated to Financials, VTV functions more like a traditional value ETF, given how cheap P/E, P/B, and P/S ratios are typical in that sector. In contrast, SEIV has 30.40% allocated to Technology, which is actually the 12th highest among the 148 U.S. large-cap value ETFs I track. It's no secret that tech stocks have outperformed the broader market since SEIV's launch, so let's add that to the list of possible reasons it's done so well over the last four years.

VTV also overweights sectors with pricing power, namely Consumer Staples (10.02% vs. 4.90%) and Health Care (15.58% vs. 12.13%), which should give it an edge in high-inflation environments. Note that inflation has been trending downward for much of SEIV's existence, but depending on the outcome of the Iran War and the direction of oil prices, that could change in 2026. According to the latest University of Michigan Survey of Consumers, "Year-ahead inflation expectations surged from 3.8% in March to 4.8% this month, the largest one-month increase since April 2025." In this scenario, I can see VTV having the edge over SEIV, and YTD through April 16, 2026, it is slightly outperforming (6.37% vs. 4.28%).

VTV Fundamental Analysis: Top 10 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for VTV's top ten holdings, which comprise 21.06% of its portfolio. I've already covered VTV's growth deficiencies relative to SEIV earlier in this review, but you might be surprised to learn that VTV also has higher P/E ratios than SEIV. For example, its 21.18x trailing P/E ratio represents a 35% premium over SEIV's 15.74x, so from a growth-at-a-reasonable-price perspective, it's not even close - SEIV wins in a landslide.

However, in VTV's defense, it does appear to be the less risky of the two. Its 0.84 five-year portfolio beta is meaningfully lower than SEIV's 0.98 figure, and although its ROA is lower, it does hold the edge over SEIV on EBIT margins (21.76% vs. 19.11%) and ROE (21.24% vs. 20.87%). Furthermore, SEIV appears to have lost the price momentum advantage that has carried it to such strong returns over the last few years. Today, its components' weighted average one-year price returns are excellent at 55.18%, but VTV isn't far behind at 46.32%.

SEIV Fundamental Analysis: Top 10 Holdings

Next is SEIV's top ten holdings list, which totals 26.44% of the fund, or about 5% more than VTV. Here, we see the strength of SEIV's big tech plays come out. Its modest allocation to Nvidia (NVDA) gives it a growth, quality, and momentum boost, as does its 2.53% allocation to Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL). These are smart holdings because even though their P/E ratios are high and their dividend yields are low, they still offer excellent value for their price. They're market leaders for a reason, so rather than outright avoid them like VTV does, SEIV's managers have chosen to keep them but constrain their weight. To me, this makes more sense and represents a bit more of an "evolved" approach to value investing.

Another advantage SEIV has is better earnings surprises, which I calculated at 8.50% based on last quarter's results. In addition, SEIV's Seeking Alpha EPS Revision Grades are better. If one were to convert the grades on a ten-point scale, SEIV's weighted average score would be 6.77/10 compared to 5.99/10 for VTV, so arguably, its components have earned their higher one-year price returns.

Investment Recommendation

VTV and SEIV are strong large-cap value ETFs that operate very differently from each other. On the one hand, VTV is more of a traditional value play with high exposure to the Financials sector and a solid performance track record that "wins by not losing." I think it will appeal to risk-averse investors looking for straightforward, low-cost value factor exposure, but one word of caution is that its index intentionally selects stocks with lower sales and earnings growth rates and return on assets. This strategy is not necessarily meant to benefit investors, but instead, it's designed to cleanly separate the CRSP Large Cap Index into Value and Growth baskets. That drawback leads me to a simple "hold" rating for VTV.

On the other hand, SEIV also has excellent value factor exposure, evidenced by its 15.74x and 11.44x trailing and forward P/E ratios. Unlike VTV, its active managers can ensure good exposure to other important factors, namely quality, growth, and momentum. It's less diversified and has a slightly higher 0.15% expense, but overall, I find its total factor mix to be superior. Still, SEIV is not without risk. It has a higher five-year portfolio beta (0.98 vs. 0.84), and it's not yet known how well its managers can adapt to a potential high-inflation market where momentum stocks are less dominant. I'm optimistic, and my caution isn't enough for me to back off my previous "buy" rating. Still, it's probably not best for risk-averse investors with shorter time horizons, so if that describes you, I'd steer you towards VTV.

Thank you for reading, and I hope you found the information in this article helpful. As always, please reach out with any questions on VTV, SEIV, or any other U.S. equity ETF you're eyeing, and I hope to keep the conversation going with you in the comments section below.

data.title: Jade Biosciences: Caution Advised Before Clinical Trial Results
data.description:
Jade Biosciences (JBIO) is a pre-revenue company that develops therapies for inflammation and patients with autoimmune diseases. Their biggest asset is JADE101, which targets the anti-A PRoliferation-Inducing Ligand (APRIL) pathway for the treatment of immunoglobulin A (lgA) nephropathy (a chronic, progressive kidney disease). This asset entered the testing phase in mid-2025.

This introductory article lays out the pros and cons of Jade Biosciences, discusses risks such as competition, and dives into the company's balance sheet. At this stage, I am rating Jade a Hold, mainly because it is not yet clear if the company will succumb to risks or if its one-of-a-kind drug might propel it above its competition.

Introduction & Timeline

Jade Biosciences' price chart is messy. A year ago, the "parent company," Aerovate Therapeutics, suffered a hit to its stock price, which tumbled more than 90%, from $860 to $58. This was because in June 2024 its only pipeline drug (AV-101 for pulmonary arterial hypertension) failed a critical Phase 2b clinical trial. Months after this, in April 2025, Jade became a public company through a reverse merger. To execute the merger, Aerovate confirmed a 1-for-35 reverse stock split and paid out a one-time cash dividend. The drop on the 52-week chart from around $100 to $10 was caused by this reverse split, not by the market. Since the end of April 2025, the stock of Jade has climbed by about 60% to $15.5.

Analysts expect Jade to start generating revenue in 2029, which is still 3 years from now. But between now and then, the company still has to undergo a lot of scrutiny.

Key Pipeline Drugs

JADE101 is Jade's main asset, targeting Immunoglobulin A nephropathy (IgAN). The total market for IgAN is estimated at $10 billion annually in the US alone, mainly fueled by high unmet needs of patients. The most anticipated event is the data for JADE101 interim Phase 1 in healthy volunteers, which is expected to be released in Q2 2026 and will determine the exact dosage that will be used in the Phase 2 trial for JADE101 in IgAN patients. This trial will begin right after the results (mid-2026) of trial 1. The data from phase 2 trials will likely arrive in 2027. After this, the drug still has to undergo Phase 3 and FDA approval, and only then can it be let out into the market. From the competitive point of view, JADE101 will face commercial drugs like Otsuka's Voyxact (sibeprenlimab), as well as developmental APRIL and BAFF inhibitors like Novartis' zigakibart and Vera Therapeutics' atacicept.

Jade also has a second drug, JADE201 (for rheumatoid arthritis), which is expected to enter testing in humans for the first time in Q2 2026. The targeted market here is huge. The global rheumatoid arthritis drug market is projected to reach over $37 billion in 2026 and approach $48 billion between 2030 and 2040. If this drug reaches the market, it will again face competition from already established drugs. JADE301 (Jade's third drug with an undisclosed target) is expected to begin first in-human tests in the first half of 2027.

Balance Sheet health

Diverse testing such as this burns a lot of cash, and Jade Biosciences doesn't yet generate any revenue. The majority of their money comes from equity offerings. In 2025 alone, they increased the number of outstanding shares by 8447%, from 817 thousand in 2024 to 71 million in Q4 2025.

These sales generate significant amounts of cash on hand, and as of December 2025, the cash on hand is $88.4 million, and $247.7 million is in short-term investments. Together, this gives Jade total liquidity of over $336 million, but developing multiple assets simultaneously burns a lot of cash. If clinical trials ramp up according to plan, spending will likely jump to between $80 and $100 million annually. Management states that this is enough to form a financial runway until 2028. However, they might not start generating revenue before 2029. Very likely, this will force Jade to create more public or private equity offerings.

For these offerings to succeed, it is of the utmost importance that all trials (mainly JADE101) are a success. If the trials do not succeed, Jade will have to postpone the timeline of when they expect to start generating revenue. An unsuccessful trial would push the stock price of the company down, possibly tumbling by more than 50%, as we saw with the "parent company." A failed trial would inevitably increase the amount of runway cash Jade needs, and if they can't find an investor, they will have to sell out millions of shares at a low price. There is also a possibility for bankruptcy, which, in this scenario, might be likely.

Pros and Cons

Jade Biosciences has one big factor playing in its favor. lgAN is a chronic disease caused by overproduction of a molecule called galactose-deficient IgA1. These molecules create complexes that accumulate in the kidneys, causing inflammation and, over time, loss of kidney function. The APRIL cytokine is a key component of this overproduction, and by neutralizing it, JADE101 aims to strike at the heart of the issue (kidney deterioration), not just treat the symptoms.

JADE101 targets the APRIL cytokine with "femtomolar affinity," making it about 750 times more potent than competing drugs (sibeprenlimab, for example). This means that JADE101 could be dosed once every 8 weeks, possibly in even longer intervals. This makes it much more convenient for patients and gives Jade a huge advantage. In the US there are roughly 150,000 to 170,000 patients who will require lifetime treatment of Immunoglobulin A nephropathy (IgAN). If Jade Biosciences manages to get JADE101 approved, they will have a strong upper hand.

Jade is entering a highly competitive space, plus they are a late bloomer. Companies like Otsuka, which manufactures and markets Voyxact (sibeprenlimab), already received FDA approval in November 2025. Jade is still years away from making their drug public and will have to persuade the market that JADE101 is superior to competitors' drugs. This is the main risk the company is facing. Furthermore, as they are pre-revenue and are relying on one single drug, if test data around JADE101 underperforms or shows safety issues, the stock is volatile and will tank.

Valuation

I compared Jade Biosciences to some of its competitors to see if they are fairly priced. All of these are pre-revenue companies in the biotechnology industry. The companies are trading at vastly different share prices (JBIO $16.2, APGE $84.8, SYRE $49.3, BMEA $1.5). The listed companies do have different potentials and are developing different drugs. Because pre-revenue companies have no earnings, standard valuation metrics like the P/E ratio do not yet exist. Instead, I looked into their balance sheets for comparison, specifically at the price-to-book ratio, to see how big of a premium the market places on Jade. They actually outshine their competitors with a highly attractive price-to-book value (JBIO 3.5, APGE 5.7, SYRE 23.9, BMEA 3.7). This low P/B value implies that investors are paying a lower premium for Jade than for other pre-revenue biotech companies. This provides a wide margin of safety, making the current valuation stripped of some risk compared to peers.

Conclusion

Jade Biosciences is a risky play. If all trials succeed, the stock price will likely skyrocket. If one trial at this stage fails, the stock will tank, and investors will lose a substantial amount of their money. Their balance sheet is stable, cash reserves of $88 million offer a stable runway, and long-term liabilities of less than a million dollars ($724K) support pre-revenue longevity.

The core advantage of Jade is the JADE101 drug, which is much more potent than the drugs competitors offer and so more attractive for patients. This one advantage is the thing that would make the stock shoot up if all clinical trials are a success. Development of other drugs like the JADE201 and JADE301 suggests that Jade is here for the long term.

My advice in the current situation would be to hold the company; thus, my rating. Navigating from the most anticipated test data results would be the smartest choice. If reducing risk is the priority, waiting for Phase 2 results of JADE101 in IgAN patients, which will arrive in 2027, might be the optimal strategy.

data.title: DYNF: Potent Factor Rotation Strategy Likely To Outperform IVV In 2026
data.description:
In my view, the iShares US Equity Factor Rotation Active ETF (DYNF), which I upgraded to a Buy in January after being neutral on it for a few years, has a strategy potent enough to outperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year and hopefully beyond.

It is worth clarifying that I have not cut my 2026 target for the S&P 500 that I released in December, even despite the Iran war (even though I mulled it over) and the oil price rally that it catalyzed, and I consider the 7,550 level achievable by the end of the year, which means an around 6% return from the level the index is hovering around as of writing this article. From the growth- and GARP-stocks-heavy DYNF, I expect a stronger result, securing its fourth year of outperformance in a row. There are three main reasons for that:

  • its impressive performance track record proving the potential of its strategy based on the macro-informed proprietary model,
  • robust factor mix of the portfolio,
  • and much stronger weighted average Quant, Seeking Alpha Analysts, and Wall Street ratings that its portfolio is offering at the moment compared to IVV.

Reason 1: History Of Outperformance Proves The Strategy's Efficiency

As explained on the iShares website, the actively managed DYNF

... seeks to outperform the investment results of the large- and mid-capitalization U.S. equity markets by providing diversified and tactical exposure to style factors via a factor rotation model.

From page S-2 of the prospectus, we know that the "proprietary model" at the nucleus of the strategy

... uses commonly-used equity style factors such as momentum, quality, value, growth, size and minimum volatility and dynamically allocates the factors, and seeks to emphasize those factors with the strongest near-term return prospects. The model incorporates two potential sources of return: long-term return premium and short-term returns from timing the cyclical behavior of each individual factor.

What I especially like is that the assessment of "the current economic cycle" also contributes to the model:

The model incorporates information about the current economic cycle as well as the valuations and recent trends for each factor to compare the relative attractiveness of each factor and seeks to guide the portfolio to tilt into favorable factors and away from unfavorable factors in pursuit of incremental returns.

All this might sound intriguing and quite promising at the same time, but does this model work? There is a simple way to assess whether its approach bears fruit or not—it is to look at the returns since DYNF's inception in March 2019. And I believe the results speak for themselves.

First, since its inception, DYNF has outperformed not only IVV but also a cohort of multifactor peers, including the Invesco Russell 1000 Dynamic Multifactor ETF (OMFL), the WisdomTree US Multifactor Fund ETF (USMF), the Xtrackers Russell US Multifactor ETF (DEUS), and the First Trust Lunt US Factor Rotation ETF (FCTR).

Second, delving deeper, we see that over the April 2019–March 2026 period, DYNF has not only delivered the highest active return in the peer group (with IVV selected as a benchmark), and, in fact, the only positive one, but also achieved fairly robust risk-adjusted returns as illustrated by the Sharpe and Sortino ratios.

Importantly, since I upgraded DYNF to a Buy January 3, it has outperformed the S&P 500 despite all the challenges stemming from the capital rotation, the software names sell-off, and the Iran war.

Reason 2: Factor Mix Fits Perfectly Into The Market Narrative

Sadly, the Strait of Hormuz situation appears complicated even though on April 17, Iran announced that the waterway is "open for commercial vessels." I ought to acknowledge here that the issue is more likely to remain a concern to monitor. Besides, some lingering effects of the supply chain chaos that ensued from the waterway's blockade, not only for crude oil but also for natural gas, fertilizers, and other commodities, will still reverberate through the global economy in the coming months.

Nevertheless, it is my point that the U.S. market is currently primarily driven by expectations that there will be no surprises on both the inflation and labor market fronts, so at least one interest rate cut will be delivered this year. And the very core of the thesis that the oil crisis would create something of a 2021–2022 situation, with prices across the economy climbing higher at a record pace, and thus prompt interest rate increases that would decimate equities, is now disintegrated as the peak escalation is more likely behind. That is to say, I think that some short-term volatility will likely be shrugged off, not inflicting much damage on the S&P 500 and not hindering it from touching 7,550 this year. In this environment, I believe growthier names with high quality and a GARP tilt are positioned to benefit, and this is exactly what DYNF is offering at the moment.

More specifically, as of April 16, there were 187 equities in its portfolio. It is worth briefly mentioning that the contingent value rights in Abiomed, which was acquired by Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in 2022, are still present. The key holding was NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), with an 8.6% weight, which also topped the list in January. Overall, 114 equities (93.7% of the net assets) that we see in the current iteration of the portfolio were present in the January version as well.

So in terms of factors, most importantly, we see DYNF offering notably stronger growth characteristics, including higher weighted average revenue, EBITDA, and especially EPS growth rate, as per my calculations.

This advantage is secured by its larger exposure to the trillion-dollar league as well as by its sector mix that is significantly heavier in IT than IVV's.

However, the usual problem we see with large-cap portfolios is that they are unapologetically about growth, with little or no regard to value. But this is not the case with the DYNF portfolio, which features a tilt towards the growth-at-a-reasonable-price style, as illustrated by the PEG (the weighted average TTM P/E divided by forward EPS growth rate), Price/Sales-to-Growth, and EV/EBITDA-to-Growth ratios.

Metrics DYNF IVV
PEG Ratio 1.045 1.235
PSG Ratio 0.576 0.571
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.034 1.112

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Besides, even though the quality factor might lose relevance during recoveries as investors demonstrate higher risk tolerance, I still think that it makes sense to maintain a decent footprint in higher-quality names regardless of the market regime, as stress factors can be unpredictable, and exposure to companies with resilient business models, industry-leading margins, and solid capital efficiency metrics can add a layer of safety. And on that front, DYNF is offering an appealing story, sporting higher adjusted ROE, ROA, and ROTC. Moreover, loss-making companies have only 0.65% weight in its portfolio vs. 1.85% in IVV. With that being said, its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability rating or higher is 2.6% lower than IVV's, but it is still impressive at 91.4%.

Reason 3: Stronger Quant, Seeking Alpha Analysts, and Wall Street Ratings

What also contributes to the high hopes I have for DYNF is that its portfolio is sporting a higher weighted average Quant score than that of IVV.

What additionally reinforces the thesis is that the DYNF portfolio also has stronger WA SA analysts and Wall Street scores.

DYNF Risks

As I have already explained in the previous article, DYNF had sluggish periods in the past. For instance, it underperformed IVV every year from 2019 to 2022. I believe that any proprietary model has its vulnerabilities, and miscalculations leading to wrong positioning or inability to react fast enough due to perplexing or rapidly changing circumstances cannot be completely ruled out.

Importantly, I might be wrong with my expectation that the Strait of Hormuz situation will not drive a new oil price rally and will not catalyze a significant reacceleration of inflation. But if I am wrong and the worst-case scenario materializes, it is reasonable to expect DYNF to underperform the S&P 500 ETF as it did in the 2022 bear market (i.e., it declined by 20.26% while IVV was down by 18.16%).

Final Thoughts

To conclude, I find it likely that DYNF will outperform the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF this year, delivering a high single-digit gain from the current level, so I maintain the Buy rating.

data.title: Intel Has Gone Too Far Too Fast (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Intel (INTC) is up 74% YTD, approaching its dot-com boom highs. The company has been boosted in an era of extraordinary demand for custom silicon as it's focused on returning to cutting edge chip manufacturing. Intel is a company we've recommended before, before switching to a hold. Since then it's substantially outperformed the market, and we're now switching to a sell.

Intel 18A/14A

Intel's announced 5 nodes in 4 years plan was designed to make the company competitive with TSMC once again.

Intel, just a few days ago, launched its Core Series 3 mobile processors. These processors are launched on the foundation of Panther Lake as processors on 18A nodes. Intel has been consistently improving its 18A yields, which is key to the company's ability to release silicon at high margins, and it's something that Intel needs to keep improving.

Intel has acquired at least one external customer with Microsoft for Intel 18AP. Expectations are that Intel will be able to acquire big customer wins with Intel 14A, which could be enormous. However, it requires that Intel do exceedingly well at 3 things.

  • Build an effective PDK for customers to transfer silicon designs.
  • Ramp cutting-edge nodes ahead of TSMC.
  • Provide customers with lower costs and effective, reliable silicon.

Moreover, TSMC isn't the only competitor; they're just winning. TSMC has pushed its capex to ~$54 billion, versus $9.1 billion for Intel. Samsung's 2nm yields are reportedly lagging, but it's clear the company is investing heavily in it. Elon Musk signed a $16.5 billion chip order with Samsung, showing Samsung's focus on cutting-edge nodes.

Then there's a potential new competitor in Japan, with Rapidus, which is targeting scaling up 2nm production in 2027. It's clear that there's enormous opportunity in cutting-edge silicon, but it's also clear that this is a competitive, high-volume industry.

Intel and AI

Intel is continuing to work to handle the changing artificial intelligence markets. However, the AI boom also has a massive risk. The demand for data center silicon has two risks that we see as incredibly substantial to Intel.

First, DRAM prices are skyrocketing. As seen in the chart above, the PC market is expected to shrink in 2026 across a variety of scenarios. That's a big price impact. Intel primarily supplies silicon to the PC and smartphone markets, so the shrinking of these markets could have a big negative impact on Intel's financials at a critical time when it needs as much capital as possible.

The second is the increasing scale of companies. Broadcom is targeting more than $100 billion in AI accelerator revenue, and Google TPUs, including a recent deal with Anthropic, are a big part of that. A decade ago, Google servers would have only used Intel or AMD CPUs. Now Google is building its own.

The scale of the largest tech companies includes Apple, which dropped Intel for its own custom silicon, saving several billion per year. On top of Google, Amazon and Microsoft are both making custom silicon as well. The growth of the TPU market is above. And it means a big move away from Intel.

Now the flip side is true: companies are chasing whatever edge they can, scaled across $100+ billion capex budgets. That means that a number of companies that might not have switched from TSMC to Intel are now looking at Intel.

Another benefit is Intel is an expert. Nvidia recently invested $5 billion in Intel along with a deal for Intel and Nvidia to work together on silicon. However, with Intel at a $350 billion market cap and a P/E of more than 100x, the company is priced for perfection and success in these markets as competition is ramping up.

Intel Financials

Financially, Intel has continued to struggle with substantial competition.

Intel had almost $14 billion in revenue, above the company's October outlook, but still down more than 4% YoY. Similarly, the company saw a more than 4% decline in gross margin YoY. Annualized EPS of $0.15 put the company's P/E at comfortably above 100x given its recent share price appreciation over the past months. The Q1 2026 outlook is $0 EPS with a 4.7% margin decline.

Intel continues to see substantial losses within its foundry division to the tune of ~$10 billion annualized. Intel once talked about not proceeding to further nodes without an external customer, and now that seems to have changed. So that's promising, but when Samsung and TSMC are winning massive external customers, it's tough competition.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is government pressure to move towards the U.S. Intel is the only U.S.-based company building cutting-edge silicon, silicon that's increasingly important for the global order. It led to the U.S. government ultimately taking a stake in Intel. We expect that to support Intel getting external customers, as companies look to garner reliable domestic supply.

Conclusion

Intel has seen its share price appreciate substantially. The company has benefited from expected demand in the market, as investors expect Intel to become increasingly relevant to the AI wave as companies search for American silicon. The company recently renounced new products on its 18A node and signed exciting partnerships with Nvidia.

However, our view is the company has run up too far too fast. Intel is trading at a triple-digit P/E of more than 100x, with substantial competition in the 2nm space. At the same time, the company has the risk that large customer tech companies are increasingly building their own silicon, which hurts Intel's CPU and GPU businesses.

The combination here is that Intel is a sell in our view at this time.

data.title: PTY: Attractive Valuation And Dividend Is Still Supported
data.description:
Overview

As the debt market continues to face headwinds, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) has continued to decline in value. At the time of my prior coverage, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation and attractive outlook at the time. Since then, the fund has continued to decline in value, and the interest rate environment has shifted. Additionally, the fund has released an updated semi-annual report that provides transparency into the earnings. Despite the headwinds of the sector, I believe that PTY remains a great opportunity for investors that want to secure a stream of supplemental dividend income.

When I previously covered PTY, the fund traded at a premium to NAV of 10.75%. Following the slight pullback in its share price, PTY now trades at a smaller premium to NAV of 4.83%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PTY continues to trade at one of the most attractive price-to-NAV levels over the last ten-year period. Even on a smaller time frame, PTY has traded at an average premium to NAV of 21.7%. Therefore, this serves as an excellent time to accumulate shares if you are a long-term income investor looking for a good opportunity in the market.

PTY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.7% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Despite the attractive valuation of the fund, I do believe that additional downside risks remain. Unfortunately, growth for the fund may be limited as long as interest rates remain elevated. The reality is that a debt-focused fund like PTY is almost entirely reliant on a healthy debt market, and since interest rates are still near the highest end of its ten-year range, the risk of defaults continues to be relevant. The fund's thinner operating margins will also limit the rate of NAV growth in the near term, which can threaten dividend sustainability.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PTY has total managed assets of $3.3B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund's primary goal is to generate a high current income through various types of income securities, so investors should understand that PTY's performance will not be correlated with the movement of the indices since the portfolio does not hold equities. Furthermore, the fund provides some global exposure, and this can be great for investors seeking a bit of diversity away from the U.S. markets. However, the majority of the fund is still allocated to U.S. positions, with the full portfolio breakdown as follows:

  • United States: 59.39%
  • France: 5.10%
  • Bermuda: 3.39%
  • Germany: 3.22%
  • Colombia: 2.90%

PTY is mostly allocated to high-yield credit securities, so the fund's success is ultimately reliant on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. This is followed by an allocation to other income-producing assets like Non-USD developed and emerging market securities, which account for 16.03% and 15.52% of assets, respectively. Expanding on this, the fund maintains an efficient allocation across different sectors.

Most notably, the fund is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 11.89% of assets. During a period where markets are displaying pessimism towards SaaS companies, this could increase the overall risk profile of the fund. Software companies are seeing their moats threatened by the capabilities and cost efficiency of LLMs like Gemini, Claude, and ChatGPT. So while it isn't specifically highlighted anywhere in fund documents, this exposure to technology positions could be negatively impacting earnings. On the bright side, the portfolio is very diversified across many other areas of the market to help reduce any sort of concentration risks.

Although most of the fund's portfolio is allocated to securities with short-dated maturities, the fund continues to display a sensitivity to the high-interest rate environment. Shorter-dated securities are typically more resilient to high-interest rates, but this has not been the case for PTY, likely due to the fund's use of leverage. For reference, the funds breakdown by maturity is as follows:

  • 0-1 Years: 22.56%
  • 1-3 Years: 19.98%
  • 3-5 Years: 24.78%
  • 5-10 Years: 19.78%
  • 10-20 Years: 8.15%
  • 20+ Years: 4.74%

The fund now has total effective leverage of 17.55% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$521.5M. Although moderate, this can still increase the overall risk profile for the fund if allocated to positions that decline in value. As we can see below, the fund has struggled to regain any sort of positive momentum since interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Since the fund is using leverage, the operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities will be thinner, making it more difficult for leverage to be used efficiently. Therefore, high-interest rates are likely to continue suppressing the share price in the near term.

Performance Insights & Outlook

Since the time of my last coverage, PTY has released an updated semi-annual report for the first half of its 2026 period. Net investment income amounted to $0.59 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.17 per share. Assuming PTY is capable of achieving a similar level of net investment income over the remainder of the current period, the net investment income level will fall at the lower end of its historical range. Net investment income consists of the interest income collected from its securities, and the decline may be attributed to the negative impact of leverage.

Furthermore, net investment income doesn't support distributions on its own, so this leaves the fund quite reliant on net realized gains to bridge that gap in its earnings. Under normal market conditions this would be a fine dynamic, but the high-interest rate environment means that the fund is very unlikely to see its positions rise, which means net realized gains will be limited. This becomes an issue since it means that PTY may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it earns, which will directly cause the NAV to erode. This is one of the reasons why the NAV has been unable to reach the highs of 2021. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $12.44 per share
  • 2021: $14.40 per share
  • 2022: $11.21 per share
  • 2023: $10.83 per share
  • 2024: $11.17 per share
  • 2025: $11.73 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $11.92 per share

So when an investor holds PTY for a long time horizon, this dynamic translates to very little upside price growth unless interest rates are low. For instance, the fund's share price is now down by 10.46% over the last ten years. While the fund did experience some upside growth in the past, these gains were eliminated as soon as interest rates were hiked to their ten-year highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 143.1% over the same time frame.

Looking forward, I anticipate that performance will substantially improve once interest rates begin to trend lower. The only challenge is that it can be hard to estimate how low interest rates will fall since the macro environment can change so rapidly. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided that interest rates would remain unchanged. There are two ways that lower interest rates will improve performance:

  1. Improve the fund's operating spread and margins for existing positions. A lower cost of debt will provide relief and reveal some new investment opportunities.
  2. The market will see less risk associated with the chance of defaults from debt-focused securities.

The issue is that the prolonged period of elevated interest rates has probably lasted longer than the market originally anticipated. This higher cost of debt has put pressure on borrowers and makes it challenging to keep up with required debt maintenance. Since PTY has an allocation to securities that are below investment grade, this increases the fund's exposure to being impacted by defaults. For reference, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 58.89%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.14%
  • Not Rated: 6.98%

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1188 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. Assuming that the fund decides to keep payouts unchanged for the next twelve months, we can assume that PTY will pay out an annual amount of $1.4256 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report for the period, PTY is on track to support the payouts with earnings. PTY reported total earnings of $0.76 per share for the semi-annual period while issuing distributions totaling $0.71 per share. So despite the variable earnings over the last few years, the fund has still managed to support its distributions.

Looking back over the payout history over the last ten years, we can see that management decided to reduce payouts in mid-2021. This could have been done in anticipation of the fluctuation in interest rates, but I think it was a good thing. Management's decision to cut distributions shows that they are proactive when assessing the macro environment, which can help preserve the NAV through periods of uncertainty. So the decision to keep distributions consistent right now can be an indication that management is confident in their ability to continue generating sufficient earnings.

Although the fund is currently supporting distributions, the coverage is still thin. In a scenario where interest rates do not trend lower or the portfolio experiences some deterioration, the total earnings may decline and threaten the sustainability of payouts. Even if earnings continue to support distributions, a scenario where they don't grow will continue to threaten the rate of the NAV growth.

Despite this, management's ability to keep payouts within a consistent range is still a notable accomplishment. This is because it allows income investors to create their own dividend growth. I ran a backtest of an original $100,000 investment, which assumes that all dividends were reinvested, but no additional capital was ever invested after that initial $100k. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting distributions, an investor would have seen their annual income nearly double over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $11,410

2025 Dividend Income: $22,673

Lastly, investors need to consider that an investment in PTY may not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 87.88% of the latest distribution was supported by net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, the fund may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that the fund remains an attractive buy at this time due to the price-to-NAV valuation and the ability to support distributions. Despite the fund's NAV not growing, the earnings still outpace the distributions being paid, which should keep the share price stable. Once interest rates start to trend lower, this should boost the fund's share price and earnings potential. The challenge is that we don't know how much longer it will take for interest rates to fall back towards their pre-pandemic levels. The fund maintains a healthy allocation towards higher-rated securities, which should help PTY remain a bit more resilient during a scenario where the debt markets continue to face headwinds. For this reason, PTY remains an attractive buy, and the ability to support distributions means that it can still serve as a powerful income compounder for long-term investors.

data.title: Ormat Technologies: The AI Investment Not On People's Radar
data.description:
Investing in the "Stranded" Solution: The Bull Case for Geothermal

Investing often requires finding solutions in unexpected places—specifically where others aren't looking. While the "herd" follows well-worn paths, a contrarian mindset identifies where the world is advancing before the shift becomes obvious. Geothermal energy represents one of these opportunities, in my opinion.

The Geothermal Landscape

Naturally, public investing options for geothermal energy are currently limited. The primary player is Ormat Technologies (ORA), the world’s only vertically integrated geothermal company. Ormat handles everything: exploration, drilling, manufacturing, and operation. Despite its dominant position, its market capitalization sits under $7 billion, illustrating how early it is, to invest in geothermal energy.

Ormat is the world’s only vertically integrated geothermal company that explores, drills, designs, builds, manufactures, owns, finances and operates geothermal power plants.

Other companies are beginning to pivot toward this space:

  • Constellation Energy (CEG): Owns "The Geysers" in California, the largest geothermal project in the U.S. (acquired via Calpine in early 2025).

  • Helmerich & Payne (HP) & Liberty (LBRT): Traditional oilfield services companies now applying their drilling expertise to geothermal heat.

  • Fervo Energy: A private leader in "enhanced" geothermal, backed by early-stage investments from players like Liberty and Devon Energy (DVN)

The Macro Problems

1. Energy Demand

The EIA projects global energy demand will grow by 50% by 2050, driven largely by Asian economies. More urgently, the IEA expects demand from AI, data centers, and cryptocurrency to double between 2022 and 2026. While the MSCI Sustainability Institute notes this is a small slice of the global 172,000 TWh used annually, the rate of growth is already straining existing grids.

2. Clean Energy Shift

Regardless of personal skepticism regarding climate change, the "prevailing winds" of global capital are moving toward decarbonization. Financial centers such as New York City are committed to monetizing carbon credits; this movement won't necessarily kill hydrocarbon energy, but it creates a sizable vacuum that must somehow be filled by "green" baseload power.

3. Geographic Constraint

The primary weakness of geothermal energy has always been its "stranded" nature. Unlike coal or gas, you cannot ship geothermal heat in a boat or train, and you can't control where the geothermal energy will be located. You must use the power exactly where the resource sits, and those resources are often in remote, unpopulated regions.

The Solutions: Why AI and Crypto Change the Game

Historically, a utility company needed to generate power near its customers. Today, technology has decoupled the location of power from the location of the user.

Solving the "Location" Problem

AI and proof-of-work mining (like Bitcoin) are the "perfect customers" for geothermal. They do not need to be located in a city; they only need a data connection.

  • Transporting Information vs. Electricity: It is expensive and inefficient to transport electricity over thousands of miles of wires. However, it is incredibly cheap to transport information via fiber optics or satellite.

  • The New Paradigm: Companies can now build massive compute centers directly on top of remote geothermal sources. The "compute" happens at the energy source, and the resulting data (the AI answer or the mined Bitcoin) is sent wirelessly to the end user.

Geothermal's Unique Edge

Unlike solar or wind, geothermal provides baseload power. It runs 24/7, regardless of whether the sun is shining or the wind is blowing. For AI data centers that require constant, steady uptime, geothermal is the only "green" solution that matches the reliability of hydrocarbons or nuclear without the threat posed by nuclear. And this is exactly what the management highlighted in their most recent earnings call in February. They are seeing growing demand from hyperscalers such as Google (GOOGL)(GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), etc., who need the constant baseload energy that wind and solar cannot provide.

Free Cash Flow

2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025
CF from Ops ($MM) 265.0 258.8 281.0 309.4 410.9 335.1

% Growth

 NA -2.6% 8.6% 10.1% 32.8% 18.5%
Capex ($MM) (320.8) (419.3) (563.5) (618.4) (487.7) (619.8)
FCF ($MM) (55.8) (160.5) (282.5) (309) (76.8) (284.7)

Based on the 2025 10-K, cash flow from operations in 2025 was hit by a deferred income tax that came due as well as several other one-time items. This should not be interpreted as a trend.

In their most recent presentation, the company projected a capital expenditure spend in 2026 of $675 million. The company continues to outspend the cash flow, which to some will be viewed negatively. To others who believe the future is geothermal, this is just a part of investing in a growth-oriented company.

Ormat Technologies Q4 & FY2025 Results

Ormat Technologies reported its annual and Q4 results at the end of February. They delivered strong performance in 2025, with total revenues climbing 12.5% to $989.6 million. This was fueled by growth in its product and energy storage segments. The company showed significant fourth-quarter momentum, reporting $276 million in revenue. This was 19.6% above 2024 and was above analyst expectations.

The core electricity segment remains the company's financial anchor, contributing $693.9 million. However, the energy storage division was high-growth, with revenues increasing 109% to $79 million following the successful commissioning of major projects like Bottleneck and Lower Valley. This growth in the energy storage division looks poised to continue.

Supported by a 1.6 GW global generating portfolio and strategic partnerships with tech giants like Google, Ormat issued an optimistic 2026 outlook, projecting revenues to cross the $1.1 billion milestone for the first time. This trajectory, backed by a 1 GW development pipeline, reinforces management's commitment to reaching up to 2.8 GW of total capacity by 2028. The size of the pipeline relative to their current capacity demonstrates their growth potential. This shows Ormat Technologies is beginning to capitalize on the critical baseload power needs of AI and data centers.

This slide from their recent quarterly presentation highlights that the demand is coming.

Ormat Technologies Metric Comparison to Utilities Sector

Right now, Ormat is rated as a Sell according to Seeking Alpha's quant system, with an overall grade of D+ for its growth metrics and a rating of F based on valuation. But is this rating earned?

Growth

When you compare Ormat Technologies to the Utilities sector, what we find is that Ormat is outperforming in key areas. Based on my financial opinion, Ormat is underperforming in less critical areas for a company that is geared towards growth. I've split the categories out below to show the ones that Ormat is outperforming in.

Growth Metrics

Metric ORA (Value) Sector Median
Revenue Growth (YoY) 12.49% 6.89%
Revenue Growth (FWD) 9.74% 6.28%
EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) 10.00% 7.71%
ROE Growth (FWD) 2.29% 1.74%
CAPEX Growth (YoY) 27.09% 12.34%

Ormat Technologies underperforms in areas that are more measurements of the bottom line. For a company that is investing in growth like Ormat, one might expect their bottom line to suffer from higher capital expenditures while their top line grows disproportionately. The question becomes, is Ormat Technologies investing in profitable growth.

Valuation

Ormat Technologies receives an F grade on valuation from Seeking Alpha's quantitative system. Ormat is currently being valued as a growth stock relative to its utility energy peers. And this is likely rightfully so. But is it too overvalued? Here are the valuation metrics where you can see for yourself.

Iran and Geopolitics

A fairly recent development is the geopolitics in the Middle East and the loss of dependable energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz. This development has widespread implications that the world won't have sorted out for a long time, I suspect. Nor do I think this conflict has been resolved, just temporarily postponed. Although the United States has a strong supply of hydrocarbons, both US data centers and other areas of the world will no doubt begin thinking longer term about how they can reach geographical energy independence as well as how to become more immune to hydrocarbon price volatility.

Conclusion: Skating to Where the Puck is Headed

Headquartered in Reno, Nevada, Ormat Technologies sits at the intersection of profitability and the "green" transition. While the U.S. has ample hydrocarbons and a renewed interest in nuclear, geothermal offers a unique opportunity for patient investors who can see where the "puck" is headed.

The puck is not at the remote geothermal well yet, and it's really not even close to it, but as AI demand continues to explode and the requirement for carbon-neutral baseload power intensifies, my suspicion is the world will have no choice.

Based on valuation and challenging growth, I rate Ormat Technologies a Hold. There are likely better investments levered to artificial intelligence in the short-term. But if you like to be diversified and have a long-term view, Ormat Technologies is a lesser known way to invest in green energy that is and will increasingly be leveraged to artificial intelligence.

data.title: Targa Resources: Growth Supports A Dividend Hike With More To Come
data.description:
Shares of Targa Resources (TRGP) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 35%. After languishing for much of 2025, shares have gained momentum in 2026 thanks to its differentiated growth profile and solid results. I last covered shares in November, when I rated the stock a “buy” given its strong underlying cash flow and growth prospects. I argued that shares were left behind because its lower dividend profile left it overlooked by traditional midstream investors, but that its growth would provide upside. That call has played out well, with shares gaining 38%, significantly outpacing the market’s 9% run. After such a run and a dividend increase, now is a good time to revisit TRGP.

Targa Boosts Its Dividend 25%

Now in the midstream sector, investors typically focus on income. Most MLPs and related companies pay substantial dividends, which has made them attractive to income-oriented investors. A pushback I have sometimes received in my bullish analysis of Targa is that it pays a comparatively low dividend. I have argued that this should not dissuade buyers, as it is in the midst of a large cap-ex program, using some of its cash flow. At the same time, it has been more aggressive using buybacks, given they are more flexible, while it would likely steadily raise its dividend over multiple years as projects come online. To that point, Targa repurchased $642 million of stock in 2025, and these buybacks reduced its share count by about 2%.

In fact, I argued in November that the company would likely raise its dividend by 20% annually through 2028. Sure enough, on Friday, Targe Resources increased its dividend by 25% to $1.25, giving shares a prospective dividend yield of 2.1%. This yield is still well below peers (often in the 6-8% range), but the pace of growth is powerful and will help to steadily narrow the gap. After facing pressure during COVID, it has since increased its dividend from $0.10 to $1.25 since the end of 2021. Over the next two years, I see its dividend rising to $1.80-$2.00. While Targa may not offer as much current income, it can generate substantial income growth, which may create a better total return package for investors.

Now, while the pandemic is (hopefully) a once-in-a-lifetime shock, it is entirely reasonable for investors to look at the dividend history and fear that while TRGP has been growing its payout during this economic expansion, the payout could be cut again if there is a downturn. In that case, current dividend growth would not be sustainable and would undermine any bullish thesis. Importantly, TRGP has built a more sustainable, diverse business via acquisitions and organic projects.

Targa Has A Resilient Model

90% of its cash flow is now fee-based, and even on the 10% that is commodity-exposed, it has hedged the majority of that exposure to lock in margins. Because of this, a 30% move in commodity prices only impacts EBITDA by about 2%. This shift towards fee-based contracting has significantly improved the stability of its cash flows, making its dividend more secure. Of course, over the longer term, if prices stay very low, upstream producers may drill and produce less, reducing long-term volumes and growth. That risk is not unique to Targa, of course.

Importantly, Targa has a solid balance sheet, which enables this cap-ex spending. It ended 2025 with leverage at 3.5x given its $17.4 billion of debt, right at the midpoint of its 3-4x target. As you can see below, it has invested billions in growing out its system while also reducing leverage. Another reason it was forced to slash its dividend in 2020 was that it had an over-leveraged balance sheet with debt north of 5x, which made it more vulnerable to a downturn.

For greater perspective, the difference between 5x and 3.5x leverage at Targa’s current size is about ~$7.5 billion of debt. If Targa was carrying ~$25 billion in debt today, I would be more concerned about its balance sheet and dividend sustainability. It now has significant financial headroom that provides more flexibility and stability for shareholders. The fact it has been able to invest over $10 billion in cap-ex and M&A while cutting leverage speaks to the benefit of its fee-based model and advantaged Permian footprint.

Targa Has A Leading Growth Profile

These benefits are showing up in its financial results, too. In the company’s fourth quarter, Targa earned $545 million even as revenue declined by 8% to $4.1 billion. Adjusted EBITDA was up 5% to $1.34 billion. For the full year, it nearly hit $5 billion, up 20%, while investing $3.3 billion in growth cap-ex. Even with its large growth spending, it generated $539 million in free cash flow, an improvement from 2024, though we will likely see a decline here in 2026 given even greater capital spending.

Its Permian volumes increased by 11% in 2025. Thanks to positioning in prime acreage and an integrated “wellhead to water strategy,” TRGP has been able to grow even faster than basin-wide production. Given the strength in production out of this basin, it is planning a new Delaware Basin processing plant while making initial plans for two more that could be opened in 2028. Once these projects are complete, we should see capex come down. Meanwhile, they will begin producing revenue, adding incremental operating cash flow. Combined, this positions Targa for meaningful inflection in free cash flow over the next three years.

This growth is unlikely to stop. Looking forward, Targa is guiding to $5.4-$5.6 billion of EBITDA in 2026, up 11% from last year. It will spend $4.5 billion on growth projects, up $1.2 billion. 2026 should represent a peak year for spending.

Targa Has A Compelling CapEx Plan

CapEx is so high as it works to build the Speedway NGL pipeline system out. This will directly connect its Permian operations to Mont Belvieu. It will have an initial capacity of 500,000 barrels/day with the scope of doubling. There is scope to double the capacity, and it should begin producing revenue by Q4 2027. For those unaware, Mont Belvieu is the key site of NGL, setting the benchmark for LPG pricing. Additionally, from here, product can travel to export terminals along the Gulf Coast for sale in the global market.

This is essential because selling to the export market is a critical underpinning of production growth in the Permian. Targa is undertaking a “wellhead to water strategy” that can carry product from the Permian right to export, a key reason it has seen stronger volume growth. Obviously, the war with Iran has caused significant disruption in global markets. Given the fact that US infrastructure is being fully utilized, I see limited benefits to Targa today, especially as Speedway is not yet operational. Over the medium term, though, this crisis, I believe, can boost the relative attractiveness of US exports as Asian buyers in particular seek to ensure predictable supply. While there is no impact on near-term fundamentals for TRGP, the events of the past two months likely boost the duration of the growth story and increase my conviction in my core view that US energy exports will continue to grow.

Once Speedway is complete, run-rate growth spending should be closer to $2.5 billion, assuming ~10% volume growth across its Permian system. This is higher than its prior estimate of $1.7 billion, as its discussions with producers point to greater production growth. These bullish conversations were occurring even before the Iran war.

Shares Remain Attractive Before Earnings

Now, Targa is slated to report Q1 results on May 7th, and analyst optimism has been building with 7 estimate increases and no cuts over the past ninety days. I expect to see solid results with continued growth in Permian flows. If anything, margins may be a bit better given March commodity volatility. However, I believe the outlook will be even more crucial. I do not expect a change in 2026 guidance; however, I expect TRGP to speak even more optimistically about medium-term growth prospects given greater appetite for US exports.

Back in November, I was targeting $5.25 billion of EBITDA and $4 billion of distributable cash flow. I now expect $5.5-$5.7 billion of EBITDA and at least $4.2 billion of DCF. That is over $19.35/share. Its $5 dividend is nearly 4x covered by DCF, speaking to how TRGP is keeping its payout low to fund its large growth program. As that growth program matures, that frees up significant cash flow for dividend hikes.

I like to see pipeline companies maintain at least 1.3x coverage, so its “potential” dividend is about $15, giving shares a potential 6% yield. Of course, with its growth program and completion of underway projects, DCF and EBITDA should continue to grow. Given its prime positioning in the Permian and for LPG exports, I view a ~5% “potential dividend yield as appropriate, or $290-$300, reflecting at least 5% long-term growth.

While this is fair value, I recognize many investors in this sector prefer current income, so I am haircutting my price target by 10%, expecting shares to trade with a discount to fair value. I believe $265 is a reasonable price target. Even after this large run, I see further upside as the medium-term outlook is very robust. I remain a buy on TRGP.
